corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: We provide empirical evidence that our pro- posed modification (i) decouples the optimal choice of weight decay factor from the setting of the learning rate for both standard SGD and Adam and (ii) substan- tially improves Adam’s generalization performance, allowing it to compete with SGD with momentum on image classification datasets (on which it was previously typically outperformed by the latter).

context_window.previous: While common implementations of these algorithms employ L 2 regularization (often calling it “weight decay” in what may be misleading due to the inequivalence we expose), we propose a simple modification to recover the original formulation of weight decay regularization by decoupling the weight decay from the optimization steps taken w.r.t. the loss function.
context_window.current: We provide empirical evidence that our pro- posed modification (i) decouples the optimal choice of weight decay factor from the setting of the learning rate for both standard SGD and Adam and (ii) substan- tially improves Adam’s generalization performance, allowing it to compete with SGD with momentum on image classification datasets (on which it was previously typically outperformed by the latter).
context_window.next: Our proposed decoupled weight decay has already been adopted by many researchers, and the community has implemented it in TensorFlow and PyTorch; the complete source code for our experiments is available at https://github.com/loshchil/AdamW-and-SGDW

top_candidates.sentence: Our experimental setup follows that of , who proposed, in addition to L 2 regularization, to apply the new Shake-Shake regularization to a 3-branch residual DNN that allowed to achieve new state-of-the-art results of 2.86% on the CIFAR-10 dataset .

For example, used AdamW to train a novel architecture for face detection on the standard WIDER FACE dataset , obtaining almost 10x faster predictions than the previous state of the art algorithms while achieving comparable performance. employed AdamW with cosine annealing to train convolutional neural networks to classify and characterize error-related brain signals measured from intracranial electroencephalography (EEG) recordings.

AdamW with the same hyperparameter setting as Adam yielded higher test set accuracy on Deep4Net (73.68% versus 71.37%) and statistically significantly higher test set accuracy on ResNet (72.04% versus 61.34%). employed AdamW to train Transformer architectures to obtain new state-of-the-art results on a wide range of benchmarks for natural language understanding.
We now discuss a justification of decoupled weight decay in the framework of Bayesian filtering for a unified theory of adaptive gradient algorithms due to .

After we posted a preliminary version of our current paper on arXiv, Aitchison noted that his theory "gives us a theoretical framework in which we can understand the superiority of this weight decay over L 2 regularization, because it is weight decay, rather than L 2 regularization that emerges through the straightforward application of Bayesian filtering." .

This result implies a preconditioner of the gradients that is given by the posterior uncertainty Σ post of the filtering distribution: updates are larger for parameters we are more uncertain about and smaller for parameters we are more certain about. goes on to show that popular adaptive gradient methods, such as Adam and RMSprop, as well as Kronecker-factorized methods are special cases of this framework.

Decoupled weight decay very naturally fits into this unified framework as part of the state-transition distribution: Aitchison (2018) assumes a slow change of the optimizer according to the following Gaussian: where Q is the covariance of Gaussian perturbations of the weights, and A is a regularizer to avoid values growing unboundedly over time.

Our experimental setup follows that of , who proposed, in addition to L 2 regularization, to apply the new Shake-Shake regularization to a 3-branch residual DNN that allowed to achieve new state-of-the-art results of 2.86% on the CIFAR-10 dataset .
We also perform experiments on the Im-ageNet32x32 dataset , a downsampled version of the original ImageNet dataset with 1.2 million 32×32 pixels images.

For example, used AdamW to train a novel architecture for face detection on the standard WIDER FACE dataset , obtaining almost 10x faster predictions than the previous state of the art algorithms while achieving comparable performance. employed AdamW with cosine annealing to train convolutional neural networks to classify and characterize error-related brain signals measured from intracranial electroencephalography (EEG) recordings.

AdamW with the same hyperparameter setting as Adam yielded higher test set accuracy on Deep4Net (73.68% versus 71.37%) and statistically significantly higher test set accuracy on ResNet (72.04% versus 61.34%). employed AdamW to train Transformer architectures to obtain new state-of-the-art results on a wide range of benchmarks for natural language understanding.

While common implementations of these algorithms employ L 2 regularization (often calling it "weight decay" in what may be misleading due to the inequivalence we expose), we propose a simple modification to recover the original formulation of weight decay regularization by decoupling the weight decay from the optimization steps taken w.r.t. the loss function.
Nevertheless, state-of-the-art results for popular image classification datasets, such as CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 , are still obtained by applying SGD with momentum .

Furthermore, suggested that adaptive gradient methods do not generalize as well as SGD with momentum when tested on a diverse set of deep learning tasks, such as image classification, character-level language modeling and constituency parsing.

The fact that Adam is an adaptive gradient algorithm and as such adapts the learning rate for each parameter does not rule out the possibility to substantially improve its performance by using a global learning rate multiplier, scheduled, e.g., by cosine annealing.

We hope that as a result, practitioners do not need to switch between Adam and SGD anymore, which in turn should reduce the common issue of selecting dataset/task-specific training algorithms and their hyperparameters.

For example, used AdamW to train a novel architecture for face detection on the standard WIDER FACE dataset , obtaining almost 10x faster predictions than the previous state of the art algorithms while achieving comparable performance. employed AdamW with cosine annealing to train convolutional neural networks to classify and characterize error-related brain signals measured from intracranial electroencephalography (EEG) recordings.

While their paper does not provide a comparison to Adam, they kindly provided us with a direct comparison of the two on their best-performing problem-specific network architecture Deep4Net and a variant of ResNet.
